topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp parchment felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the official notification. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo in the otherwise silent library. The words, stark and unforgiving, confirmed my worst suspicion: the prestigious astrophysics scholarship, the one I had poured every waking hour into securing, was not mine. It was allocated to a student named Clara Vance. I reread the fine print, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I pushed the paper away, the sound sharp in the quiet space. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the polished floor. "This is absurd," I muttered to the empty aisle, my voice tight with disbelief. I walked out of the library, the afternoon sun suddenly feeling like a spotlight on my foolishness.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance letter, the ink blurring slightly as my vision swam. The details were unmistakable: the name listed, the specific funding amount—it was all there, meticulously laid out, pointing directly away from me. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I clenched my fists so tightly my knuckles turned white. How could this happen? I walked over to the window, the cityscape blurring into an indistinct wash of grey and steel. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass, breathing in deeply, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. "They made a mistake," I whispered, the words sounding hollow and weak even to my own ears. I turned away from the view, the realization settling in my stomach like a cold stone.

[story 3]

When Professor Albright finally called me into his office, the air in the room seemed to thicken, suffocating. He slid the document across the mahogany desk. My breath hitched. The official designation was clearly marked, and the name associated with the award was not mine. I didn't move for a long moment, just absorbing the cold, hard facts laid bare before me. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously low, pitched just above a murmur, but carrying an undeniable sharpness. "I need an explanation," I demanded, leaning forward, my posture rigid. My gaze locked onto his, unwavering, daring him to offer a plausible lie. I felt a furious heat rising in my chest, a desperate need to dismantle whatever deceit had led to this outcome.
